items.question: My sister ___ speak four languages.
explanations: Correct! 'Can' has one form for every subject and takes the base verb: 'She can speak'.
'Can' is not used with 'be' like this.

items.question: When I was young, I ___ climb trees easily.
explanations: 'Can' is present; this is about the past.
Correct! 'Could' expresses general past ability: 'I could climb trees'.

items.question: ___ you pass me the salt, please?
explanations: Correct! 'Could you…?' is a polite request.
'Are able' would need 'to' and is unusual for a request.

items.question: I'd love ___ play the guitar.
explanations: Correct! Use 'be able to' after another verb: 'I'd love to be able to play'.

items.question: The exam was hard, but I ___ finish in time.
explanations: For one specific past success, 'could' is not used.
Correct! One successful past action takes 'was able to': 'I was able to finish'.

items.question: She ___ drive; she failed her test twice.
explanations: Correct! The negative of 'can' is 'can't' (cannot): 'She can't drive'.

items.question: I ___ reach you all morning — your phone was off.
explanations: 'Couldn't' is possible but the perfect form fits 'all morning' up to now better.
Correct! A perfect meaning needs 'be able to': 'I haven't been able to reach you'.
'Can't' is present, but this refers to a period up to now.

items.question: Correct this: 'He can plays the piano.'
options: He can to play the piano.
explanations: Correct! A modal is followed by the base verb: 'He can play'.
Use the base verb, not '-ing', after a modal.

items.question: We ___ get tickets at the last minute — we were lucky!
explanations: 'Could' isn't used for one specific past success.
Correct! 'Managed to' fits one successful past action: 'we managed to get tickets'.

items.question: Choose the sentence about GENERAL past ability.
options: I was able to solve the puzzle yesterday.
When I was ten, I could swim a kilometre.
I managed to catch the bus.
I've been able to fix it.
explanations: Correct! 'Could' expresses general past ability: 'I could swim a kilometre'.
This is a present perfect ('be able to'), not general past ability.
